poem:
The house settles into its familiar creaks.
Another Tuesday arranges itself around the edges.

Each stair remembers the weight of small feet.
The banister holds decades of passing hands.

A spike of pain behind my left eye.
The aspirin bottle rattles nearly empty again.

Dinner needs to be less bland, more spicy.
The family barely looks up from their phones.

Even the roses grow spiny this late in season.
Everything that blooms eventually learns to defend itself.

Today I did not break.
title: Sharp Things in Order

poem:
The plant needs water again today.
Same yellow leaves as yesterday.

I arise at six thirty sharp.
Coffee first, then the morning paper.

The chair creaks when I sit down.
Twenty-three years in this same spot.

Clair from next door waves through glass.
Her husband died last spring sometime.

The cider tastes like artificial autumn.
Nothing tastes quite right anymore.

A cigar would complete this morning.
But the doctor said no more smoking.

Some habits die harder than others.
title: Saturday Morning Routine

poem:
The house feels larger without her footsteps.
Every room echoes what isn't there.

My heart keeps its steady rhythm.
The doctor says this is normal.

I sit on the bench outside daily.
The wood remembers our conversations.

Nothing helps the way it should.
Time moves but doesn't heal.

Fresh flowers wilt in three days.
I buy them anyway each week.

The Welsh hymns she hummed while cooking.
I cannot remember the words anymore.
title: The Same Small Rooms

poem:
The audio equipment hums in the ICU.
Another night shift bleeding into dawn.

The twins arrived at three this morning.
One made it through the first hour.

Her liver numbers came back elevated.
We schedule the difficult conversation for noon.

Grief sits in the family waiting room.
It knows these halls better than I do.

I wield my stethoscope like a compass.
It points toward things I cannot fix.

The cider vinegar smell of disinfectant clings.
Some mornings the weight just wins.
title: The Morning Rounds

poem:
The train arrives seventeen minutes late again.
Platform announcements echo through empty skulls.

House prices climbed another eight percent this quarter.
The mortgage broker stops returning your calls.

You have shown up for thirty-two years straight.
The performance review mentions areas for improvement.

A sword would solve this more efficiently.
HR policies prohibit most meaningful solutions.

Swoll bros at the gym discuss crypto portfolios.
Your lower back reminds you about yesterday.

I swore this job was temporary once.
The pension vests in eighteen more years.
title: Five Forty Seven Daily

poem:
She arose at dawn for another dissertation day.
The coffee was cold before nine.

Her advisor leant back in his leather chair.
Three years of work needed major revisions.

The department promised equal treatment for all candidates.
Some labs got funding, others got hope.

Her thesis was ideal in theory only.
Reality had different requirements for completion.

Each petal of her research fell away.
The hypothesis couldn't hold its own weight.

They gave her a participation medal instead.
The defense date remains unscheduled indefinitely.
title: What We Earned

poem:
The house payments stack like blueprints.
Three decades of mortgage left.

You enter the shop before sunrise.
The familiar weight of routine.

They teach precision in trade school.
Nobody mentions the ache afterward.

Once you leapt at overtime shifts.
Now your back argues with standing.

The metal stock arrives each Tuesday.
Same grade, same supplier, same price.

The lathe runs smooth as clockwork.
Your hands know what they cannot fix.
title: The Workshop Grows Quiet

poem:
The crane operator called in sick again.
Another equipment failure to document.

The mouse under the server rack died.
Small problems become larger ones.

The theme of this quarter is efficiency.
Management speaks in measured tones.

The modem blinks red in the corner.
Connection terminated without warning.

Behind the hedge someone is laughing.
The sound does not reach here.

I wedge the cable back in place.
Nothing changes in the display.
title: Signal Lost

poem:
I stare at the kitchen counter again.
The dishes multiply when I look away.

I unbox another delivery from somewhere online.
More things I thought would help.

The broken cabinet door has reached its point.
Everything falls out when I open it.

I find one glove under the couch cushions.
Its partner disappeared months ago.

Hello, I practice in the mirror.
My voice sounds smaller each time.

The kids want jello for lunch again.
I have stopped explaining why no.
title: Another Day At Home

poem:
I stare at the foundation where it stood.
The concrete remembers what came before.

Smoke still clings to my jacket sleeves.
Three days since the call came in.

The spine of the staircase survived somehow.
Metal twisted but still reaching upward.

Each scene tells the same quiet story.
What burns away was never permanent.

Things end. Other things do not.
title: What Remains After Fire

poem:
I stare at the sediment layers again.
The grant money runs out next month.

This mound holds nothing but disappointment.
Forty years of digging yields dust.

The alien feeling creeps in at night.
My colleagues publish while I excavate silence.

Every penny spent on failed expeditions.
The university questions my methods now.

The bones I thought significant were eaten.
Time devours even the most careful theories.

The ocean will claim this site eventually.
Some mysteries prefer to stay buried.
title: What We Leave Behind

poem:
I stare at the new patient intake forms.
Another back injury from improper lifting.

The smell of smoke clings to his jacket.
Construction work takes its toll eventually.

His spine shows the typical compression patterns.
Thirty years of bending without proper form.

We slide through the basic range assessments.
He winces but doesn't complain much.

He swore he'd be back to work immediately.
They always say that on day one.

The surge of progress comes in week three.
Small victories build toward something larger.
title: The Weight of Moving Forward

poem:
The morning arose with flour already coating everything.
Another day of measuring what cannot be measured.

Each point on the recipe means something different.
The customers want what they cannot name.

Everything moved according to the timer's demands.
The dough knows better than I do.

The mould grows in places I cannot reach.
Health inspectors dream in spreadsheets and violations.

Mocha sells but the machine breaks weekly.
Repair costs more than three days of profit.

The mochi never sets quite right here.
Some traditions refuse to travel this far.
title: The Shape of Things

poem:
The house sits empty for another month.
Showings down to one per week.

I roast the same chicken every Sunday.
My mother asks when I am coming home.

The market reached its breaking point in spring.
Now October feels like giving up.

The world keeps building houses somewhere else.
My commission check bounces twice.

Every blond woman looks like my ex-wife.
She got the good listings in the divorce.

I block her number but the silence stays.
The mortgage payment comes due tomorrow anyway.
title: Everything Gets Smaller

poem:
The audio crackles over the intercom again.
Room 302 needs assistance with mobility.

I walk past the house where I grew up.
Now it holds strangers and their sorrows.

Everything turns eventually in this place.
Night shift becomes day shift becomes night.

The sunny window faces east toward nothing.
Mrs. Chen asks about her daughter again.

Another patient hits their slump by noon.
The morphine helps but never quite enough.

Behind each skull lives someone's entire world.
Today I remembered how to hold it gently.
title: The Morning Shift

poem:
The audio crackles through decades of dust.
Static fills the spaces between words.

Every store downtown sells the same silence.
Shelves lined with things nobody needs.

Birds roost where the tower once stood.
The frequency finds its way home.
title: Signal and Static

poem:
I arise at six for morning affirmations.
The routine requires discipline and presence.

The coast calls but content deadlines loom.
Engagement rates drop without fresh posts.

My salon appointment books three weeks out.
The waiting list grows longer each month.

A shark tank rejection still stings sometimes.
The pitch deck sits unopened on desktop.

I snack between sponsored product shoots today.
The camera adds pounds they always say.

The sauna promises detox and inner peace.
Nothing feels clean anymore.
title: Weekend Self Care Rituals

poem:
Everything I know about loss fits in boxes.
The records tell me nothing about grief.

This house holds forty years of documentation.
Each room catalogued but never lived in.

Every pound of paper weighs the same.
The scale cannot measure what is missing.

History accumulates in careful mounds.
Time sorts itself into manageable piles.

The old system begins to moult its usefulness.
Change arrives whether I document it or not.

The files have started growing mould in corners.
Some things cannot be preserved.
title: The Weight of Things

poem:
The crane operator shows up late again.
Nobody says anything about the overtime.

They pound rebar until their shoulders ache.
The foreman counts every bent nail.

A ghost crew worked this site before.
Their accidents still hang in the air.

The concrete stays moist in the forms.
Everything depends on perfect timing now.

We hoist the last beam into place.
Something solid finally holds.
title: Heavy Lifting

poem:
The audio from last night's recordings loops.
Same lesson plans need different voices.

The crane outside has been there since Tuesday.
Construction makes focusing harder for everyone.

All the plans made in August feel distant.
September always changes what you thought you knew.

The classroom stank of disinfectant and hope.
Tomorrow requires the same steady showing up.
title: The Morning Routine

poem:
Another night stare at empty hallways.
The cameras see nothing worth recording.

Alone with the hum of fluorescent lights.
My rounds echo through sleeping buildings.

Years make you agile in the dark.
Every shadow becomes a familiar neighbor.

Awake while the city finally rests.
Dawn creeps through windows like relief.

The morning shift arrives on time.
title: Four Thirty Morning Shift

poem:
The house deposit sits untouched again.
Rent money disappears into theory.

Every trail of research leads nowhere.
The bibliography grows longer each day.

Going crazy was never the plan.
Sanity costs more than tuition.

Mind goes blank during office hours.
Advisor schedules another meeting next week.

Even the dwarf planets have purpose.
My thesis proposal remains unfinished.

Gamma rays travel across infinite space.
Some destinations are never meant to arrive.
title: Everything Leads Somewhere Else

poem:
The foundation settles. Every stone finds place.
Nothing stays level in this house.

Every marriage becomes a trial eventually.
The evidence accumulates in small rooms.

Hard to admit what we've been avoiding.
The ceiling creaks with old secrets.

Children leave behind their antic years.
Toys gather dust in forgotten corners.

The attic holds what we cannot release.
Some things find their proper home.
title: What We Keep Upstairs

poem:
The house sits empty for another week.
Three showings scheduled, one actual visitor.

The market feels acute right now.
Everyone waiting for rates to drop.

Women buyers call with detailed questions.
Men buyers want to see it tomorrow.

I guide them through the same rooms.
Point out the crown molding, updated fixtures.

Every couple thinks they are the queen.
Of negotiation, of getting the deal.

The buyer finally signs at asking price.
Another commission, another closing planned.
title: The Listing Agent

poem:
I stare at the espresso machine again.
Same routine. Different morning.

The score matters less than the paycheck.
Rent is due in three days.

I serve another latte to another suit.
They never say thank you.

The super raised rent for building improvements.
No improvements have appeared yet.

My back gets sorer with each shift.
Standing ten hours does that.

The day shred my last nerve completely.
Tomorrow I clock in anyway.
title: The Daily Grind

poem:
The stone keeps crumbling in my hands.
Every castle needs better mortar.

The ridge where we pitched camp floods.
Authenticity costs more than expected.

The chime of steel on steel rings hollow.
My sword arrived bent from overseas.

We dance around the fire like always.
Nobody mentions the divorce papers.

My lance splinters against the quintain.
Some battles you can still win.

Even cardboard armor has its weight.
title: Medieval Dreams at Forty

poem:
Morning came and I arose reluctantly.
The building waited in its silence.

Each light I switched revealed more mess.
Fluorescents hummed their tired song.

I mined the trash bins methodically.
Same debris as yesterday's forgotten hours.

Someone hummed a ditty near the elevators.
Gone before I reached that floor.

The hallways stretched dimly toward dawn.
My cart wheels squeaked the same rhythm.

Everything looks dingy under these bulbs.
Tomorrow I will do this again.
title: The Night Shift Again

poem:
The pitch deck arose from months of work.
Nobody cares about another productivity app.

Every trail leads to the same investor meeting.
They smile and say they'll circle back.

Being the champ of your own delusions.
The team still believes in the vision.

Even a panda needs bamboo to survive.
Our runway ends in three weeks.

Time to hedge against total failure.
The co-founder already updated his LinkedIn.

Another horde of twenty-somethings with big dreams.
We join the quiet cemetery of good ideas.
title: Everything We Almost Had

poem:
The scale arose from the bathroom floor again.
Numbers don't lie about what we become.

Skip lunch means more room for dinner later.
The stomach learns to speak in smaller voices.

Guilt arrives with every bite we take.
It knows our weaknesses better than we do.

The mirror shows where everything will bulge.
Truth lives in the spaces between our clothes.

Sometimes success comes down to pure fluke.
Today the pants fit without holding breath.
title: Small Victories Count Too

poem:
The coffee arose at five thirty again.
Same cup, same counter, same silence.

Her style used to matter more.
Now it's just clean clothes, brushed teeth.

The slime under the high chair returns.
She scrapes it off without thinking.

Tomorrow she will do this again.
title: Morning Routine Maintenance

poem:
I stare at the survey markers again.
The boundaries were always there.

They hound me about setback requirements.
Municipal code enforcement never rests.

Every nimby meeting ends the same way.
Progress dies in committee rooms.

The property is lined with old fencing.
Previous owners marked their territory clearly.

New linen curtains block the view.
Some things you learn to live with.
title: Property Lines

poem:
The night shift means you stare at monitors.
Nothing changes but the timestamp.

Every company has the same tenet about integrity.
They put it on posters by the break room.

Someone reported a theft from the supply closet.
I write it in the log like everything else.

The cameras see everything that doesn't matter.
title: What We Take

poem:
The crane lifts boxes from the truck.
Nothing left but address changes now.

This house held more than we remember.
Empty rooms echo different than full ones.

These walls witnessed small daily failures.
Coffee rings and nail holes tell stories.

The sheds out back store someone else's dreams.
Previous tenants leave invisible marks everywhere.

Each shelf once held books we thought mattered.
Now they hold dust and forwarding addresses.

The sheep in the neighbor's yard keep grazing.
Some things stay while others learn to leave.
title: What Remains After Moving

poem:
The house fills with sounds I cannot name.
Sleep becomes theoretical.

Everything feels giant when you are small.
The world expands in uncomfortable directions.

Patience is grown through repetition.
Days blur into indistinguishable segments.

Time falls through the gully between moments.
Rest becomes a distant memory.

Small fish like guppy survive by hiding.
Survival requires different strategies now.

The world turns gummy and soft.
Nothing holds the shape it used to.
title: THINGS GET SMALLER

poem:
The house feels bigger now with half gone.
Everything echoes differently in empty rooms.

I loved the way mornings used to be.
Now coffee tastes like borrowed time.

There's nothing left to prove to anyone.
The lawyers have already done their math.

We used to watch every movie together.
The couch still has two worn spots.

My voice sounds strange when nobody answers.
Phone calls end before they really start.

The magazine says this year's all about vogue.
I'm learning how to dress for one.
title: After the Divorce Papers

poem:
I stare at the runway markers again.
The radio stays quiet.

Another training mound of paperwork waits.
My coffee has gone cold.

The pilot certificate renewal sits unsigned.
Everything requires more forms now.

The engine cools between lesson blocks.
Students cancel more often these days.

I feel lowly among the commercial crews.
They don't look back anymore.

The whole system has gone loopy.
I teach people to fly nowhere.
title: Another Day at Altitude

poem:
The audio equipment fails during third period.
Students stare at silent speakers.

I water the plant by the window.
Its leaves curl despite my efforts.

Steam rises from my coffee cup.
The lesson plans remain unfinished.

The tales we tell about progress.
How measurement becomes everything we know.

The maths doesn't add up anymore.
Thirty students, one underpaid professional.

My hasty corrections pile on the desk.
Tomorrow I will try again.
title: The Variables Always Change

poem:
I stare at the whiteboard again.
The numbers still don't add up.

Sandy from accounting quit yesterday.
Her resignation sits on my desk.

The salvo of investor emails continues.
Each one wants different metrics.

The slope of our burn rate steepens.
Three months of runway left.

My solid business plan feels hollow.
The team pretends not to notice.

I bought a sloop once, before this.
Now it rots in someone else's marina.
title: All These Beginnings

poem:
Every house on the street sits empty.
The market has shifted again.

Great location they said last spring.
Now the signs stay up longer.

The metro expansion was supposed to help.
Three years behind schedule already.

Theft from construction sites keeps happening.
Insurance premiums climbing with each claim.

My basic tenet was always location matters.
Even that feels uncertain now.

Client responses have grown increasingly tepid.
Nobody wants to make offers anymore.
title: Property Values Declining Again

poem:
Every house has its secrets behind doors.
The mechanisms tell their own stories.

A stern client waits while I work.
Their impatience changes nothing about metal.

Speed matters less than understanding the tumblers.
Rushing breaks more than it opens.

My tools slide into position with practice.
Years of this and still some resist.

The lock begins to swelt under pressure.
Heat and force where finesse should go.

No spell or charm works on steel.
Just knowledge that sometimes isn't enough.
title: The Right Combination

poem:
I stare at the plate like evidence.
Everything looks the same under fluorescent light.

What gets eaten first reveals character.
The rest just fills the space between hunger.
title: What Gets Consumed

poem:
The audio crackles through static at three AM.
Nothing but numbers and distant voices.

Every alien frequency sounds like loneliness.
The same patterns repeat across empty channels.

An angel would know which signal matters.
Instead there is only white noise and waiting.

My ankle aches from sitting in this chair.
The right frequency always finds you eventually.
title: Signal From Above

poem:
I stare at the quarterly numbers again.
Nothing adds up the way it should.

The team building dance begins at nine.
Everyone pretends enthusiasm works.

My badge swipes through another floor.
The elevator knows where I belong.

Being agile means moving fast toward nothing.
The methodology has its own momentum.

In the glade between meetings I breathe.
Five minutes before the next presentation.

Your grade depends on factors beyond performance.
The system grades itself highly.
title: Performance Review Season

poem:
The house listing expires at midnight tonight.
Everything fits in boxes now somehow.

The train schedule changes next month again.
Distance becomes a number on paper.

Your final grade arrived in yesterday's mail.
Four years reduced to letters and debt.

The rough draft sits unfinished still.
Cover letters all sound the same.

Every drama seems smaller from here now.
The real world opens its quiet door.

Tomorrow starts whether you're ready or not.
title: Moving Day Logistics

poem:
The crane sits idle in the yard.
Another day without a call.

He chats with Tommy by the fence.
Same conversation as yesterday.

His hands still know the clasp by feel.
Some things don't leave your muscle memory.
title: Heavy Machinery at Rest

poem:
I plant seeds in March dirt.
Nothing shows for weeks.

The apple tree bloomed late this year.
Still no fruit worth counting.

Alpha varieties promise the most.
Catalogs lie about everything.

Seedlings stretch aloft toward weak sun.
Most will die anyway.

My photo album shows last season's harvest.
Tomatoes red as hope.

The soil gives amply to those who wait.
Waiting is the hardest part.
title: Growing Things Take Time

poem:
The crane sits broken in the distance.
Metal rusts while we wait for orders.

Alert means nothing when it never stops.
The sirens blend into background noise now.

Every rogue wave looks the same eventually.
You stop counting after the third flood.

Ready is a word people use.
Before they learn what ready costs.

React too slow and the moment passes.
React too fast and you waste resources.

We rearm because that's what we do.
The enemy was always going to win.
title: Six Ways to Prepare

poem:
The morning call arose before dawn.
Another kitchen flood to diagnose.

She heated pasta water while explaining.
The overflow started around midnight.

The basin cracked along familiar lines.
Thirty years of settling does this.

Everything stays moist down here.
Concrete sweats what it remembers.

An oasis forms in flooded basements.
Water finds its level eventually.
title: Water Finds Its Level

poem:
They teach you to stare without looking.
First lesson never leaves your bones.

The sound of footsteps means everything.
You learn to count them like heartbeats.

Slime grows on forgotten safe house walls.
Nobody comes to clean these places.

You slink through crowds like water.
Invisible becomes your second skin.

The extraction goes slick as planned.
Tomorrow you disappear again.
title: Five Ways to Move

poem:
The audio from upstairs never stops.
I keep chopping anyway.

I tried explaining flavor profiles again today.
Nobody listens to technique anymore.

The basin fills with yesterday's mistakes.
Water runs cold while I scrub.

Basic skills take years to develop properly.
Most people want shortcuts instead.

Fresh basil wilts if you wait.
Sometimes timing saves everything.
title: Kitchen Counter Confessions

poem:
The house payment clears on the fifteenth.
Another month closer to something resembling ownership.

Coffee tastes stale by nine thirty.
The same mug sits on the same desk.

Seven years at this company now.
The parking spot knows my car better than home.

The sedan makes the same sounds lately.
Forty thousand miles of strip malls and traffic lights.

My setup mirrors everyone else's cubicle exactly.
We bought the same desk lamp from the same catalog.

The servo motor in the garage door died yesterday.
I press the button knowing nothing will happen.
title: The Daily Commute

poem:
Another patient points to the stone filling.
Says it hurts when she chews.

The molar shows hairline fractures under light.
Thirty years of grinding will do that.

Insurance claims this procedure is wrong somehow.
They want three more forms by Friday.

The waiting room magazines show perfect crops.
Everyone smiling with impossible teeth.

Lunch is cold broth from a paper cup.
The next appointment is already running late.

My student loans are why I'm still broke.
Some problems don't have clean solutions.
title: What the Tooth Told

poem:
The crane stands motionless in morning fog.
I check my map again.

The outer trail curves away from town.
My boots know this path by heart.

The river runs parallel to my doubt.
Water finds its way regardless.

A tiger lily blooms beside the bridge.
Summer promises what autumn takes.

The fiber of my pack strap frays.
Everything wears thin eventually.

The cider stand closed three miles back.
Some roads lead nowhere worth going.
title: The Long Way Around

poem:
Another stone thrown at the window.
Management says document everything now.

They teach you to watch for patterns.
Nobody mentions how patterns watch back.

The meter runs whether anyone comes.
Time moves the same in empty halls.

Every lefty signature looks suspicious tonight.
Trust becomes a luxury we cannot afford.

Theft happens in the spaces between looking.
What disappears was never really ours.

The building settles into quiet resignation.
We guard what has already been lost.
title: What We Cannot Keep

poem:
The house plans sit unfinished on the desk.
Blueprints for a life that won't be built.

A robot vacuum runs the empty office.
Cleaning around the scattered dream fragments.

The front entrance needs redesigning again.
Clients keep changing their minds about doors.

The world demands structures that last forever.
We design for thirty years if lucky.

Glory was supposed to come with the awards.
Instead there are invoices and code violations.

Every story needs a solid foundation first.
Mine keeps shifting in the unstable ground.
title: The Framework Holds

poem:
You stare at files until names blur.
The cases pile without end.

Another match between child and hope.
The system grinds its slow machinery.

At some point you stopped counting wins.
The losses weigh more anyway.

Dreams float past like distant boats.
You watch from shore, making notes.

Today they let you adopt new protocols.
Small victories in endless wars.

Something good is afoot at last.
The phone rings with better news.
title: The Long View

poem:
The crane sits idle in morning rain.
Coffee tastes like yesterday's disappointment.

Court papers came for the divorce hearing.
She wants half of everything we built.

Another chunk of overtime gets eaten up.
Bills arrive faster than the paychecks.

Even my foreman calls me a chump.
The foundation holds despite everything cracking.
title: Heavy Machinery Days

poem:
I stare at the same patch of sky.
The coordinates never change.

Tonight I mount the telescope again.
My hands know every adjustment.

The comet appears exactly on schedule.
Some things still follow their predicted paths.
title: Fixed Points Above

poem:
The coffee arose from yesterday's grounds again.
Another morning measuring what remains.

The snail crossed the kitchen window.
Progress exists in smaller increments.

The bills stack themselves without assistance.
Order persists despite our best efforts.

The swamp of daily maintenance never drains.
But today the dishes are finally clean.
title: Morning Inventory

poem:
The house needs repairs we cannot afford.
Nobody mentions this during breakfast.

I think the promotion went to someone else.
The email arrived while I was in traffic.

They claim the restructuring creates opportunities.
My desk faces the parking lot now.

The new hire asks weird questions about everything.
I show him where we keep supplies.

We pivot toward uncertain market conditions.
The coffee machine breaks on Tuesday.

My head feels dizzy during the presentation.
The numbers never quite add up right.
title: After the Third Meeting

poem:
The meeting starts with sharing our audio testimony.
I listen to voices that sound like mine.

Each person tells the same story underneath.
Different details, same ending we all know.

Some find healing through sport and routine.
I still avoid the gym on weekends.

We are sworn to this circle of truth.
Anonymous doesn't mean we hide from ourselves.

Standing at the shore of another day.
The water looks different when you're clear.

Ninety days sober feels like ninety years.
Tomorrow I will try for ninety-one.
title: The Long Way Back

poem:
The toast burns while I check my phone.
Another opening shift at five thirty.

I shine the espresso machine handles first.
The store stays empty until seven.

Every drink needs the right spice blend.
Customers never notice the difference anyway.

The snide comments start around lunch rush.
Karen wants her latte extra hot again.

Some people are just swine about everything.
They leave their cups on every table.

My spine aches from the bent-over work.
Six years of this and nothing changes.
title: Morning Rituals Fail Again

poem:
Said adieu to the wife this morning.
Another three weeks ahead.

The roads stretch endless through Nebraska.
Radio static fills the cab.

Dispatch crackles about a clash ahead.
Traffic backed up for miles.

Been driving staid routes for decades.
Same truck stops, same faces.

Sadly realized I missed her birthday.
Called from a Pilot station.

Sandy answered instead of her mother.
My daughter sounds so grown.

The miles don't feel as heavy tonight.
title: The Long Way Home

poem:
The heart wants what it cannot name.
We listen for something that sounds true.

Every sound carries its own weight.
Money talks but says nothing useful.

Money flows where attention goes.
The nosey ones ask better questions.

Being nosey is just caring sideways.
Honey draws more than vinegar ever could.

Honey sweetens what cannot be fixed.
Even coney rabbits know when to hide.

The coney burrows deep and waits.
Some holes lead nowhere but down.
title: What We Chase Instead

poem:
The bread sits untouched beside cold coffee.
Another early morning at the kitchen table.

Court begins in two hours today.
My opening statement needs more work.

Sorry seems like such a small word.
When someone's life hangs in the balance.

Sport was never my strong suit anyway.
I preferred books to baseball fields.

The sword of justice feels heavy sometimes.
Especially when you're holding it alone.

I am sworn to defend the innocent.
Today I might actually succeed.
title: The Advocate's Morning

poem:
The market arose from nothing this quarter.
Everyone said it would turn around soon.

Each house sits empty for months now.
The staging furniture collects dust quietly.

Another deal will close next week maybe.
The buyers always change their minds.

A moose wandered through the open house.
Nobody came to see the property.

Every lead feels loose these days.
The phone stops ringing after lunch.

Even the goose by the pond left.
The for-sale signs lean into winter.
title: The Property Chain

poem:
The foundation feels like stone today.
Nothing moves in the expected direction.

I enter the same commands again.
The screen blinks back unchanged.

The system should be tuned by now.
Every frequency still sounds wrong.

I knelt before the altar of code.
My knees remember harder floors.

The intel arrives in fragments.
Someone else solved this years ago.

At the inlet between despair and breakthrough.
The signal finally comes through clean.
title: Signal Through Static

poem:
The audio from practice runs thin tonight.
Another parent films their child's routine.

My words to the coach sound measured.
The other mothers nod and smile back.

She earned that medal through winter mornings.
Six AM ice time builds character slowly.

Her bag is laden with tomorrow's gear.
The car smells like determination and sweat.

Some victories fit perfectly in small hands.
title: First Place Ribbon

poem:
The morning blade makes its first slice.
Another section measured and marked.

The county court meets about water rights.
Everyone knows who wins these things.

The grain elevator wears its crown.
Same rust, different year showing through.

The crops tell you everything you need.
The soil tells you what you get.

Each season plays the same chord.
Hope tuned to the frequency of rain.

The old tractor starts to croak.
Still three more fields to finish.
title: The Harvest Reports

poem:
The slate arrives blank every morning.
Drawing lines that might hold weight.

The phone rings during site visits.
Contractors always need more time.

You prove the foundation can bear load.
Still the client questions every beam.

The prime location sits empty still.
Zoning board meets again next month.

You prune back unnecessary flourishes.
Clean lines serve their purpose better.

The prude committee rejects bold designs.
Another year building other people's boxes.
Function follows what they'll allow.
title: Clean Lines Only
Property Lines
poem:
The house sits where it always has.
Nothing changes on this block.

Alert notifications pile up on the desk.
Another rezoning hearing postponed.

Alien concepts like community input sessions.
The same faces object to everything.

The alley behind Main Street needs repaving.
Fifteen years of proposals gather dust.

Tomorrow's meeting might actually happen.

title: The Weight of Things
poem:
The stone sits where you left it.
Heavy things don't move themselves.

You learn what people carry in.
The weight never gets lighter.

Another patient calls to binge their grief.
Same story, different Thursday.

The tissues lined up on your desk.
Each box emptier than the last.

Fresh linen on the couch again.
Someone else's tears dried there.

Nothing alien about human pain.
It all sounds the same eventually.

title: The Daily Round
poem:
I stare at the kitchen counter again.
Same crumbs from yesterday's toast.

The phone rings during lunch prep.
Telemarketer asking about my car warranty.

The doorbell chime means package delivery.
Another thing I ordered and forgot.

I close the dishwasher for the third time.
The day folds into itself like laundry.

This cycle will repeat tomorrow morning.
Small victories count as progress here.

title: The Waiting Room
poem:
I stare at the appointment card again.
The receptionist schedules around me.

They found a small mound last month.
The ultrasound tech said nothing helpful.

The examination room stays unlit until needed.
I practice what I will ask.

The newly hired doctor seems confident enough.
Her handwriting is still legible.

The queen of diamonds falls from my sleeve.
I have been here three hours.

This venue hosts the same conversation daily.
Nobody ever gets the answer they want.

title: Foundation Work
poem:
The foreman tells us to stare at blueprints.
We know the concrete will crack anyway.

The basement stays moist through winter months.
Water finds its way despite our efforts.

The joints never get tight like they promise.
Everything settles. Everything gives way.

We drive each nail to the point of bending.
The wood splits when you push too hard.

Twenty years we built houses for other people.
Our own foundation sits on rented ground.

The crew talks about unity over coffee.
We clock out alone in separate trucks.

title: What We Keep Inside
poem:
The piano bench holds thirty years of lessons.
Students come and go like seasons.

This house echoes with scales at three.
Empty rooms remember better days.

My teaching style adapted over decades.
Now I wonder what I actually taught.

The whole profession feels different somehow.
Parents text instead of calling now.

Maple leaves collect on the front steps.
No one rakes them anymore.

The rifle sits in dad's old closet.
I never learned to use it.

title: The Sound of Things
poem:
The audio booth hums at three AM.
Another session that nobody will hear.

Opera singers warm up in Studio B.
Their voices reach notes I cannot afford.

I loath the way silence fills this room.
Between takes there is only breathing.

Castle moats once kept invaders out.
Now we build walls from white noise.

Toads croak outside the loading dock.
Even they have something to say.

The client wants a toady for yes-man work.
At least the levels are finally clean.

title: Five Ways to Mark Time
poem:
I stare at addresses until they blur together.
The route never changes but the houses do.

Each stamp tells someone's small urgent story.
I press them down like prayers onto paper.

Letters stack in perfect postal geometry.
Order is the only thing that makes sense.

Every stain on my uniform maps another day.
Coffee, rain, the residue of other people's mail.

I stand at the same corner every morning.
The mailbox waits like an old friend.

Some things arrive exactly when they should.

title: The Waiting Room Chronicles
poem:
I stare at job postings again.
Same companies, different Tuesday.

I eat alone most days now.
Savings account gets thinner.

Every place requires five years experience.
Entry level means nothing anymore.

The whale in my chest grows heavier.
Another rejection email arrives.

I waive the application fee sometimes.
Hope costs more than money.

The wafer crackers taste like cardboard.
This is what giving up tastes like.

title: What Remains Standing
poem:
The house sits where we left it.
Foundation poured in better times.

Everything built comes down eventually.
Your hands know this truth.

The mural on the bedroom wall stays.
Small hands painted flowers there.

Divorce papers use cruel language.
The law requires such precision.

Each chunk of marriage gets divided.
Assets split down the middle.

What's left clumps together somehow.
We learn to call it home.

title: The Morning Shift
poem:
The crane outside lifts another beam today.
My shift ends when the sun starts.

The pines behind the parking lot stay green.
Winter doesn't touch everything here.

You develop a sense for who won't make it.
Their breathing changes first.

I've seen this scene too many times before.
The family always asks the same questions.

Someone in room twelve begins to snore.
At least that means they're still breathing.

The work continues whether you sleep or not.

title: Another Evening Down
poem:
I stare at the wall clock.
Four thirty feels like midnight.

The telly drones about distant wars.
I change channels to find silence.

Every theme song knows my loneliness.
The house echoes with familiar jingles.

Teens walk past my window laughing.
Their voices fade toward somewhere important.

I remember camping in that old tepee.
Your hands steady on the tent stakes.

I am tired of counting empty hours.
The day surrenders to another day.

title: The Long Way Home
poem:
The regulars stare at their reflections in glasses.
Tuesday feels like any other Tuesday.

The conversation goes round to familiar complaints.
Same voices saying the same things.

Every story becomes a mound of worn details.
Nothing changes except the weather outside.

She found her keys under yesterday's receipts.
Time to lock up and count the till.

The last customer leaves drunk and apologetic.
Another shift ends with quiet satisfaction.

title: After the Last Call
poem:
The audio message plays while I pack.
His voice still sounds like home.

Every store receipt gets divided now.
Two of everything, half of nothing.

The hotel clerk asks for one key.
I take two anyway, old habits.

Money talks but says the wrong things.
It counts what cannot be measured.

Women understand the weight of starting over.
We carry it like we carry everything else.

Tomorrow I buy my own coffee maker.

title: What Everything Requires
poem:
The house needs work again.
Something always does.

Sunday roast burns while I check estimates.
The smoke alarm knows my schedule.

Basement walls stay moist despite the dehumidifier.
Forty-three hundred dollars later.

Posts on neighborhood forums ask the same questions.
Everyone pretends they have answers.

The contractor gets bossy about timeline changes.
I nod and write another check.

Everything costs more than they said it would.
Money disappears like heat through old windows.

title: Evidence Locker Tuesday
poem:
I stare at the same case files.
Nothing changes between coffee and lunch.

The phone rings with another dead end.
Someone saw nothing again.

Every crime blends into the next one.
The motives all sound familiar now.

They found urine in the alley.
DNA results take six weeks.

The victim's mother hums a dirge.
I write down what she remembers.

Under the birch tree they planted memorial flowers.
The case goes cold like all the others.

title: Six Ways to Miss
poem:
The session starts with talk of peace.
Everyone wants what they cannot name.

A beach vacation might help, they say.
Sand between toes fixes nothing permanent.

We track the same patterns weekly.
The couch holds familiar weight.

Something will crack if we push harder.
I nod and write notes carefully.

They stack their problems like dishes.
Each one balanced on the last.

Every breakthrough needs a whack of truth.
Today we run out of time again.
